posts.caption:
@hiromimusic’s relationship with the piano has always been intuitive. Born in Hamamatsu, Shizuoka, in 1979, the Japanese pianist and composer was already performing with the Czech Philharmonic by the age of 14. Three years later, after meeting Chick Corea by chance in Tokyo, she was invited to join him on stage the following night.
Since then, Hiromi has built one of contemporary jazz’s most animated careers, moving between solo piano, electric fusion and small-ensemble work with a style that is both technically formidable and emotionally unguarded.
In May 2026, Hiromi performed a number of her songs at @miumiu Jazz Club in Tokyo held at Dance Hall Shinseiki. Following her performance, the virtuoso pianist and composer spoke with @georgepistachio about  the unorthodox teachings of her childhood tutor Noriko Hikida.
Read more at the link in bio 📲
📸 Portrait: Hiromi at Miu Miu Jazz Club. Photography by @sshotakonoo

posts.caption:
Contracted to Vogue from 1981 to 1989, American photographer @sheilametzner’s poetic visual language, achieved in part by the laborious, almost alchemical, Fresson printing method, is synonymous with fashion photography from that decade. Born in 1939, the photographer’s portraits of people such as Uma Thurman, Robert Mapplethorpe and Willem Dafoe, alongside commissions for brands including Ralph Lauren, Fendi and Shiseido have been the subject of several monographs and exhibitions.⁠
⁠
C’est Chic, a new exhibition at @lagalerierougeparis in Paris pairs Metzner’s work with that of photographer, Lillian Bassman (1917-2012). Although working decades apart, this curatorial proposition suggests that the pair have much in common. At the link in bio, Adam Murray speaks with Mentzner about her photography 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Sheila Metzner and Lillian Bassman: C’est Chic. © Sheila Metzner. Courtesy of La Galerie Rouge⁠
1. Sheila Metzner, The Passion of Rome, Fendi, From Life, 1986⁠
2. Sheila Metzner, Fashion, From Life, 1988⁠
3. Sheila Metzner, Uma. Patou Dress, 1986⁠
4-5. Sheila Metzner, Joko Passion, From Life, 1985⁠

posts.caption:
Zoë Kravitz’s street style is built on pieces most people already own, the difference is knowing which ones.
Baby tees, slip skirts, worn-in trousers, tiny sunglasses, ballet flats, vintage bags, oversized knits, and the occasional perfectly tailored vest. Nothing feels forced, yet every look feels unmistakably hers.
Rather than chasing trends, her wardrobe revolves around a handful of recurring silhouettes that she returns to again and again, mixing vintage, designer, and everyday basics in a way that feels effortless.
We’ve sourced similar pieces inspired by some of Zoë’s most recognizable off-duty looks.
Shop the edit through our OneOff profile linked in bio. ⭐️ @oneoff.world

posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.

posts.caption:
Even though the @bafcsm 2026 show closed a few days ago, there are still looks I can’t stop thinking about. I’ve been reflecting on why the CSM graduate show stays with me much longer than other fashion shows. Perhaps it’s because I’m witnessing the very first collections of emerging designers.
CSM is known for producing prominent designers—from @jgalliano to @AlexanderMcQueen—who have presented their debut collections on its runway. Through fashion, these designers express their backgrounds, cultures, and the messages they wish to convey—expressing personal narratives into fashion.
I now look forward to following their next chapters with sincere admiration and support. Swipe right for highlights from the show.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.

posts.caption:
Madonna showed up to Times Square last night with no warning, a Grindr partnership, and an outfit that did all the talking. The look? A custom Dolce & Gabbana chiffon teddy with lace details over a satin balconette bra, a waist cincher, tulle gloves, and sheer hold-up stockings. Lingerie-as-performance-wear done at the highest level — and honestly, who else could pull this off on a street stage in the middle of NYC.
The free show was part Pride Month celebration, part Confessions II teaser (the album drops July 3, sequel to her 2005 Confessions on a Dance Floor). She played Hung Up. People lost it. And for what it’s worth, Times Square is supposedly where she landed as a broke 20-something with $35 to her name — so there’s a poetry to it.
The blush-and-pink palette mirrors the album artwork directly. She’s been building this visual world piece by piece since Coachella, and last night felt like the moment it all clicked.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @madonna
#Madonna #DolceGabbana #Grindr #TimeSquare #A2Zfashionmag

posts.caption:
Marlo Kelly is on the cusp of bona fide international stardom. The angel-faced Australian actor stars in the Emmy Award-nominated sci-fi series #3BodyProblem, and speaks to BAZAAR from Budapest, where she is preparing to start shooting season three. From a school production of ‘Macbeth’ and ‘Home and Away’ to her current role as “the scary lady from the show”, Kelly recounts the humble – and serendipitous – start of her acting career. “It feels like an idea that I was born with,” shares the self-described “theatre nerd”.
Through the link in bio, see @marlokelly photographed back home in Australia and continue reading the digital cover story.
Marlo Kelly wears @ralphlauren and @poloralphlauren on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
“It is the luckiest thing that’s ever happened in my life,” says @marlokelly of the moment a talent agent signed her following her performance as Lady Macbeth in a school production. Upending the usual trope, the actor’s parents were supportive of her prioritising art over academics, on occasion. “One time [in year nine], I was worried about exams as well as a play,” she recalls. “And [my dad] was like, ‘don’t worry about your exams – just learn your lines.”
At the link in bio, continue reading BAZAAR’s interview with the #3BodyProblem star.

posts.caption:
When internet boyfriend #JoshOConnor hit the red carpet for his upcoming blockbuster #DisclosureDay, he was wearing a @dior T-shirt embroidered with the cover of ‘Around the World in 80 Days’, one of Jonathan Anderson’s favourite books.
But he’s just the latest in a growing list of fashion insiders, celebrities and tastemakers quite literally wearing their reading lists. From #Dior’s literary motifs to #Booktok and return of yearning, the literary flex has become one of fashion’s most visible status symbols. Scroll on for more behind the trend.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)

posts.caption:
How do design and culture inform one another? At Bazaar Dialogues 2026—in association with The Bicester Collection (@thebicestercollection) and hosted at Bicester Village (@bicestervillage)—Shalini Misra (@shalinimisraltd), Ashiesh Shah (@ashieshshah) and Elena Foguet came together for ‘Visual Language Meets Cultural Intelligence’, an insightful discussion on the power of storytelling, cultural fluency and creative expression in an increasingly connected world.
📸: Noorunisa (@noorunisa)
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia #BicesterVillage #TheBicesterCollection
posts.alt_text:
In her first official interview after ‘Dhurandhar’, Sara Arjun (@saraarjunn), in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved), opens up about the conviction that shaped her role under Aditya Dhar (@adityadharfilms), embracing pressure as a quiet blessing, and revealing that acting has always been her only plan with no plan B. She also gushes about wanting to steal Rekha’s iconic saree wardrobe for a day.

With the new summer resort collection inspired by jet-set destinations like Ibiza, The Hamptons, Cannes and Capri, Louis Vuitton (@louisvuitton) brings forward vacation-ready pieces and breezy fabrics that capture effortless elegance, all under the artistic direction of Nicolas Ghesquière (@nicolasghesquiere).

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
At the second edition of Bazaar Dialogues, in association with The Bicester Collection (@thebicestercollection), guests gathered at The Apartment, Bicester Village (@bicestervillage), for an engaging conversation on ‘Discovery as the Key Currency of Luxury’. Fashion designer Anamika Khanna (@anamikakhanna.in), Clothsurgeon (@clothsurgeon) founder Rav Matharu (@matharu_rav) and I Was A Sari (@i_was_a_sari) co-founder Consuelo Funari explored how craftsmanship, innovation and conscious creation are redefining the meaning of luxury today.
📸: Noorunisa (@noorunisa)
#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia #BicesterVillage #TheBicesterCollection

posts.caption:
Since last year, Zegna has adopted a new strategy for staging shows: while designer Alessandro Sartori’s winter runway remains a fixture of the Milan calendar, he’s taken his summer outing on the road à la the Cruise shows megabrands like Chanel and Dior have long used to generate buzz and engage top clients.
This time around, Zegna turned to Los Angeles, becoming the latest luxury player to stage a big show in the US as it aims to extend its winning streak in the market, a bastion of resilience amid a wider luxury slowdown.
Satori has a remarkable talent for exploring classicism without it feeling outmoded. This time around, he took inspiration from La Villeggiatura — the Italian concept of villeggiare or retreating to a seaside or countryside villa for a prolonged stay. The results were at once proper and easy, uptight and soft.
Read Angelo Flaccavento’s full report on Zegna’s Menswear Spring/Summer 2027 collection at the #linkinbio
📷 @zegna
🎥 @poeticallypunk
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Business of Fashion on June 06, 2026. May be an image of ‎one or more people, suit, magazine, poster, dinner jacket, blazer and ‎text that says '‎BOF پن The BoF Podcast Conner Ives is Buildinga a Business With Instinct‎'‎‎.

posts.caption:
Born in the leafy enclave of Bedford, New York, designer Conner Ives, a self-professed “country mouse,” grew up in a household that taught him two things early: that quality is worth protecting, and ambition is worth following.
In his first year on the BA at Central Saint Martins, a garment from a school project — a duchess satin duster coat adorned with swans — was requested and worn by model Adwoa Aboah to the 2017 Met Gala. The moment announced him to the industry before he graduated, but back at school, the reception was rather cool:
“I remember my tutorial after [the Met Gala], being sat down and told, ‘It’s nice that you can make dresses for people’ – reducing doing the Met Gala as a 20-year-old first-year BA student to that – ‘but school has to come first,’” Ives recounts.
Now, almost six years into building his label, the designer is navigating what it takes to turn creative instinct into a functioning business. His label began with one-of-a-kind reworked vintage pieces and deadstock materials — a proposition that gave the clothes their character, but was not always easy to translate into the wholesale system.
This week on The BoF Podcast, @ConnerIves joins BoF CEO and founder @ImranAmed to discuss what it means to build an independent fashion business without losing the instinct that made the work resonate in the first place.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
📷 James Anastasi

posts.caption:
Zara’s Bond Street flagship has an upscale new look. The 3,185-square-metre store on the corner of London’s Oxford Street and New Bond Street, which reopened Thursday after a five-month renovation, is the latest example of Zara’s steady effort to elevate its image and customer experience.
Larger spaces in prime locations have been key to Zara’s strategy in recent years. The brand has been refurbishing its top stores, creating a more experiential, upmarket environment as it strives to differentiate itself from cut-price disruptors like Shein and attract shoppers priced out by luxury players over the past few years.
BoF's Marc Bain (@marcbain_) explores how the flagship reflects the brand’s effort to elevate its image and differentiate itself from budget fast-fashion players. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @zara

posts.caption:
While brands often point to EMV and MIV — which assign a dollar value to earned media exposure — as indicators of a given marketing moment’s success, many are realising that these numbers cannot be looked at in a vacuum.
Rather than laser-focusing on vanity metrics, brands are looking to a broader variety of measures to provide a more comprehensive picture of how a media moment not only gets people talking, but influences how shoppers feel about a brand.
BoF's Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) explores how these go-to marketing metrics are evolving to measure more than just the volume of online conversation. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @gap, @gucci

posts.caption:
On 27 May, designers, brand leaders and sourcing executives from @Reformation, @Coach, @KateSpadeNewYork, @Revolve, @LoefflerRandall, @Margaux, @RagAndBone, @ProenzaSchouler and more gathered at @HotelChelsea in New York to explore why Portugal has become one of the most compelling answers to the supply chain disruptions reshuffling global footwear production.
Moderated by BoF’s @YasmineDahlberg, the discussion brought together Paulo Gonçalves of @APICCAPS, Ana Correa of @WGSN and BoF 500 member Patricio Campillo of @CampilloOfficial to unpack what’s driving brands towards Portugal and what it takes to build supplier relationships grounded in craft, trust and longevity.
Read more #linkinbio
📷 BFA

From sharing the pregnancy news and setting up the nursery to celebrating their baby shower and cherishing all the little milestones in between, the post feels like a deeply personal glimpse into a chapter filled with love, anticipation and joy.

The two chose the sacred serenity of Ujjain’s Mahakal Temple over anything extravagant, seeking blessings together on the special day. The celebration felt simple, peaceful, and deeply meaningful. In a world obsessed with grand gestures, there’s something incredibly romantic about moments that feel this grounded and intentional. Because honestly, isn’t this what we’re all looking for? A love that feels calm, pure, and real.
📷: Juhi Bhatt

posts.caption:
Is this the ultimate quintessential @prada collection?
This collection is all about the layers of being a woma, both inside and out. Inspired by the idea of the “vagabond” woman, Prada’s pieces blend multiple eras , whether traveling the world or exploring her own personal journey, Prada’s collection feels deeply personal and complex. It’s a mix of old and new, with each piece telling its own story.
The collection brings back classic styles from the 1940s and 1950s, like full skirts, velvet dresses, and elegant capes. These pieces have a vintage feel but are given a modern twist with bold accessories and unique textures. For example, the beautiful capes were made with luxurious materials like tweed, fur, and brocade, creating a timeless, yet fresh look. The rich argyle tights paired with skirts and dresses gave the collection a playful, smart vibe.
What makes this collection stand out is the way Prada mixes different styles and textures together. The coats have a strong, military-inspired look with fur details, while the outfits also include more delicate, feminine touches like floral prints and intricate embroidery. Accessories, like sailor hats and book-shaped bags, add a sense of mystery, making us wonder about their deeper meanings.
Whether it’s the sexiness, the brocade, or the subtle nods to historical themes like wars or nursing uniforms, there’s always something unexpected in her shows.
Prada’s work is never just about the clothes, it’s about the feelings and stories they evoke. This collection explores who women are today, their struggles, joys, and everything in between. It’s a reflection of how women put themselves together, whether feeling powerful or vulnerable.

posts.caption:
The world's most famous blonde bombshell, perched on playground equipment, absorbed in a book. The studio make-up and lighting is gone; as are her shoes. It's 1955 and a summery glow radiates from her exposed limbs.
The woman, of course, is a 29-year-old Marilyn Monroe, captured by American photojournalist Eve Arnold in Long Island, New York. Through Arnold's lens, the Hollywood icon is quiet, contemplative and natural. Is Monroe aware of the camera? That's up for debate.
Arnold's photo tells a lesser-known story of Monroe; an avid reader who had a personal library of more than 400 books spanning poetry, plays, philosophy and dense literature like "Ulysses." And no, the book wasn't a prop, said Michael Arnold, grandson of the photographer who died in 2012. "Eve was just setting up her cameras, and she saw that Monroe got it out and was reading it, waiting for her to get ready," he said to CNN.
Look closer, and Monroe is on the last pages — where the protagonist's wife in the novel, Molly Bloom, explores female sexuality in an unpunctuated stream of consciousness. "With her choice to be seen reading the end of 'Ulysses,' Monroe was clearly making a knowing point," wrote leading feminist art historian Griselda Pollock in her 2016 essay on the photo, published in the Journal of Visual Culture. It was "an identification perhaps at so many levels with the words, the spoken words of an uneducated woman, allowed to have an inner and a sexual life, and to have the final say," added Pollock.
📸 : Eve Arnold Estate/Courtesy National Portrait Gallery
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on June 05, 2026 tagging @maggierogers, @cazziedavid, and @katieholmes. May be an image of one or more people, makeup and text.

posts.caption:
Before the likes of Telfar Clemens, @laquan_smith, or the late Virgil Abloh became fashion industry forces, Patrick Kelly blazed a trail of his own.
In just the six short years when his formal ready-to-wear line was actively in production, Kelly landed a six-page spread in ELLE magazine, maintained a robust clientele that included everyone from @madonna to Cicely Tyson and even Princess Diana, and became the first Black designer to be inducted into what is now known as the Federation de la Haute Couture, France's governing fashion body.
"Love, Patrick: Nothing is Impossible," a documentary focusing on the designer, will premiere at several film festivals later this summer and offers an intimate look at Kelly's career, charting his unlikely success and tragic death at 35 due to complications from AIDS.
The movie features rarely seen clips from his high-energy fashion shows across Atlanta, New York, and Paris, that starred supermodels like @patcleveland @naomi Campbell and @the_real_iman, and interviews with those who worked alongside him.
"What really struck me was the overall plot of this project – that a Southern boy from Vicksburg, Mississippi, made it so big in the world of fashion. That's the type of underdog story that I love," said Ryon Horne, who along with his brothers Byron and Tyson directed "Love, Patrick."
"Something that (Kelly's partner) Bjorn Amelan says in the movie is that Patrick smiled a lot, but there was something behind that smile," Horne continued. "And in this film you will find out what that truly was. We want people to find out about the real Patrick Kelly … not just the bullet points."
📸 : John van Hasselt/Sygma/Corbis/PL Gould/Images Press/Getty Images

posts.caption:
This is @dualipa's week.
Not only because she is our Look of the Week, but because the next seven days – if a swirl of reports are to be believed – will be bookended by her nuptials.
On Sunday, just as north London was being flare-painted red at a parade for triumphant local soccer team @arsenal after winning the english premiership season, the pop star and her partner, British actor Callum Turner, headed to the quiet of west London, where Lipa's bridal whites would be kept pristine, for a secret civil ceremony at Old Marylebone Town Hall. The pair will reportedly next head to the Italian island of Sicily for a more flamboyant wedding party later this week.
The bar for bridal glamour was set high with her sophisticated, low-key register office look. Lipa descended the town hall steps hand-in-hand with Turner in a custom couture @schiaparelli skirt suit complete with "personalized gold bijoux buttons," per the brand. The singer's hips were padded, her hands were gloved and her face artfully shaded by a giant hat designed by @stephenjonesmillinery that was lined with golf leaf. Turner meanwhile was dressed in a navy double-breasted suit by @ferragamo.
Lipa, who is usually styled by @lorenzoposocco was never going to be your standard bride. She's a disciple of boom-boom fashion – animal print, fur, strong tailoring and glitz that flirts with garish. Her taste is a blend of '80s silhouettes and accessorizing principles refracted through the lens of modern designers such as @matthieu_blazy, @simon_porte_jacquemus and @chemena Kamali.
📸 : Associated Newspapers Ltd/Shutterstock
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on June 05, 2026 tagging @maggierogers, @cazziedavid, and @katieholmes. May be an image of one or more people, makeup and text.

posts.caption:
When photographer Greg Brennan spotted Kate Moss in a fur coat at the bottom of a fire exit, cigarette in hand, he knew he'd stumbled across something special.
The year was 2007, and what he didn't realize is that one of the resulting photos — not even his favorite from the night — would become an emblem of the supermodel's "party era" and the best-known image of his near-four-decade career.
The mid-aughts photo's enduring appeal is, partly, its mundanity. In that quiet, unguarded moment, Moss was just like any other 30-something having a night out on the town.
And yet she is perhaps the only person who could appear that put-together while being ambushed in a stairwell. "It's kind of a mixture between a ballerina and Janis Joplin," Brennan said. "It's very rock 'n' roll."
Not all was as it seemed, though.
📸 : Greg Brennan

posts.caption:
When Icelandic Chinese musician Laufey dropped her latest music video last month, starring a cast of mixed white and Asian celebrities, the internet went wild.
"Laufey collecting wasians like pokemon," read one social media comment, using a portmanteau to describe people of mixed white and Asian descent. "WASIAN AVENGERS," another comment cheered.
She is among those who have publicly embraced the Wasian label — which has helped many people who self-identify as such to feel more seen.
This heightened attention coincides with a growing fascination in the West around Asian entertainment and culture (think the Netflix smash hit "KPop Demon Hunters," the recent Chinamaxxing trend, K-beauty products, and even Labubus). Increased visibility has crossed over into the real world, with "Wasian meetups" taking place earlier this May in San Francisco and New York City.
For some, it was a powerful and positive experience to see themselves represented and to connect with others.
But the meetups have also triggered a heated debate about race and identity that has reached a fever pitch online in recent weeks. The controversy has raised questions about who is represented, and why — as well as what it means to be multiracial in America.
Tap the link in @cnn’s bio for more.
📸 Nicole Mago/Warren Fu/Quentin Nguyen-duy
📹 Quentin Nguyen-duy

posts.caption:
In an era where fashion imagery often feels overproduced and instantly forgettable, Kate Moss proves that true editorial power has never been about perfection. It’s about presence.
Shot through the cinematic lens of Mert & Marcus, Belle de Nuit unfolds like a fever dream of glamour, mystery, and temptation. Moss moves effortlessly between elegance and rebellion, embodying the kind of magnetic ambiguity that fashion has spent decades trying to replicate.
Every frame feels suspended between fantasy and reality. There are no distractions, no gimmicks—just the enduring power of a supermodel who understands that the most compelling images are the ones that leave something unsaid.
Nearly a decade later, the story remains a reminder of what great editorial should do: create a world, tell a story, and make you want to stay a little longer.
Editorial: Belle de NuitMagazine: W Magazine, March 2017Starring: Kate Moss & Luka IsaacPhotography: Mert Alas & Marcus PiggottFashion Editor/Stylist: Edward EnninfulHair: Sam McKnightMakeup: Charlotte TilburyManicure: Lorraine GriffinProduction: North SixSource: W Magazine, March 2017

posts.caption:
Haider Ackermann continues to redefine the Tom Ford woman with a collection that feels powerful, sensual, and impossibly cool. Sharp tailoring meets fluid silhouettes, while rich textures, supple leather, and jewel-toned accents create a wardrobe that is both commanding and seductive.
From impeccably cut suiting and sculpted outerwear to second-skin dresses that celebrate the body, every look balances strength with intimacy. The collection embraces Tom Ford’s signature allure while introducing Ackermann’s own refined sense of drama—severe yet soulful, polished yet instinctively sexy.
Collection: Tom Ford Pre-Fall 2026Creative Director: Haider Ackermann

posts.caption:
Few celebrities reinvented their style as dramatically as Michael Jackson.
In the 1970s, during his early years with the Jacksons, his wardrobe reflected the era: flared trousers, wide collars, colorful prints and the kind of disco-influenced tailoring that defined the decade. He looked like a product of the 70s, but even then there was already a sense of performance in the way he dressed.
Everything changed in the 1980s. As his music became bigger, so did his image. Military-inspired jackets covered in embroidery, crystal embellishments, gold details, armbands and gloves transformed him into something more than a pop star. Michael understood that fashion could become part of the performance itself. Many of these looks would go on to become some of the most recognizable stage costumes in music history.
In the 1990s, his style became more refined and structured. Sharp tailoring, monochromatic outfits and cleaner silhouettes often replaced the excessive glamour of previous years. At the same time, he continued experimenting with futuristic elements, combining military influences with sleek materials, metallic details and silhouettes that sometimes felt closer to science fiction than traditional menswear.
By the 2000s, his appearance changed once again. As he became increasingly thin, oversized jackets, loose trousers, long coats and layered garments became a larger part of his wardrobe. The proportions grew bigger, creating a silhouette that felt both protective and theatrical.
What makes Michael Jackson’s style evolution so fascinating is that it never stood still. Most artists become associated with one signature look. Michael created an entirely new visual identity almost every decade, turning fashion into another form of performance alongside his music.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on June 06, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of one or more people, makeup, magazine, poster, newspaper, book and text that says 'Kate Moss reminds us, how an Editorial is done. CULT®'.

posts.caption:
By 2004, Britney Spears was no longer just pop’s biggest star, she had become one of the most talked-about women in the world. Her Blender magazine editorial captured that exact moment. Gone was the girl-next-door image that launched her career. In its place was a more confident, provocative and self-assured Britney, embracing a new chapter both personally and professionally.
The shoot perfectly reflected the mood of the mid-2000s: low-rise denim, leopard prints, smoky eyes and unapologetic glamour. But beyond the fashion, the editorial marked a shift in how Britney was perceived. She was no longer being presented as America’s sweetheart — she was creating her own image, on her own terms. The photos felt bolder, more mature and more rebellious than the carefully polished pop-star persona audiences had known before.
Looking back, the editorial feels like a snapshot of a turning point. It captured Britney at the height of her cultural influence, when every appearance, outfit and magazine cover became headline news. More than twenty years later, the images remain a reminder of a moment when Britney Spears wasn’t just following pop culture — she was defining it.
Editorial — Blender Magazine, 2004
Styling — Kurt & Bart,
Hair — Laurentirus Purama
Makeup — Fran Cooper

posts.caption:
Looking back at Balmain Spring/Summer 2011, it’s easy to understand why the collection became so influential. Under Christophe Decarnin, Balmain was operating at full volume: sharp shoulders, tiny dresses, distressed denim, military details, crystal embellishments and enough attitude to make every look feel larger than life.
What made this era of Balmain so exciting was that it never tried to be understated. The collection embraced glamour, excess and confidence without apologizing for any of it. Every outfit felt designed for someone who wanted to be seen. The clothes weren’t necessarily realistic, but they were unforgettable.
More than a decade later, the collection feels surprisingly relevant again. Fashion has slowly moved away from overly polished minimalism and back toward statement dressing, strong silhouettes and a little bit of chaos. The embellished jackets, micro shorts and rockstar energy that defined Balmain in the early 2010s suddenly feel very aligned with fashion’s current obsession with glamour and personality.
Whether you love it or hate it, this was a period when fashion wasn’t afraid of being loud.
Collection — Balmain Spring/Summer 2011 Ready-to-Wear
Designer — Christophe Decarnin
Brand — Balmain
Presentation — Paris Fashion Week Spring/Summer 2011
Models — Including Abbey Lee Kershaw, Sasha Pivovarova, Anja Rubik, Isabeli Fontana and others

posts.caption:
Amy Winehouse embodied everything, Westwood had spent decades celebrating: individuality, rebellion, and the refusal to fit in.
Long before “punk” became a fashion trend, Vivienne Westwood built her career around outsiders. Amy was exactly that. With her towering beehive, winged eyeliner, tattoos, ballet flats, and unapologetically chaotic glamour, she created a style that felt entirely her own. It wasn’t calculated, and that’s precisely why it mattered.
Westwood admired people who challenged convention, and Amy did it effortlessly. She mixed vintage dresses with punk attitude, combined old Hollywood references with London’s underground music scene, and made imperfection look cooler than perfection ever could. In many ways, she represented the spirit of Westwood more authentically than many models who walked her runway.
Their connection became even more visible in 2011 when Amy appeared on the cover of Blitz, wearing Vivienne Westwood shortly before her death. After Amy passed away, Westwood publicly spoke about her admiration for the singer, describing her as beautiful, talented, and uniquely original.
What made Amy so special wasn’t that she followed fashion—it was that fashion followed her. Designers, stylists, and entire generations tried to recreate the effortless cool she possessed naturally. While many celebrities wore Vivienne Westwood, Amy Winehouse lived the values behind the clothes.
That’s why she remains one of fashion’s most enduring muses: not because she was styled into an icon, but because she became one on her own.

posts.caption:
A laboratory can make a diamond.
But can it make history?
For decades, the natural diamond industry has focused on rarity, grading and certification. But Nicolas Chrétien believes the real advantage of natural diamonds lies somewhere deeper: their stories.
The newly launched World Diamond Heritage List aims to identify the 100 diamonds that have shaped human history — reminding the world that some diamonds are more than gemstones; they are cultural symbols.
💬 Do you think heritage is natural diamonds’ strongest advantage? Tell us below.

posts.caption:
Russia has introduced new labelling requirements for synthetic stones set in jewellery, requiring clearer disclosure and restricting the use of several terms traditionally associated with natural gemstones.
The move forms part of Resolution No. 657 and is designed to strengthen consumer transparency and reduce confusion in the marketplace.
But beyond the regulation itself lies a much larger industry conversation:
How should synthetic stones be described, marketed and disclosed to consumers?
As markets around the world continue to refine disclosure standards, the debate around terminology, transparency and consumer understanding is far from over.
💬 Should lab-grown diamonds continue to be marketed using the word “diamond”?

posts.caption:
Turquoise isn’t a new gemstone.
But it is entering a new phase.
Long associated with heritage jewellery and artisanal craftsmanship, turquoise is increasingly appearing across fashion-led luxury, couture styling, and colour-rich jewellery collections.
What makes it interesting isn’t rarity.
It’s recognisability.
Its colour, natural matrix patterns, and unmistakable identity make it one of the few gemstones consumers can instantly recognise across a room.
As luxury continues shifting toward design, storytelling, and individuality, turquoise is quietly becoming a gemstone worth watching.
The bigger question is:
Is turquoise simply having a moment?
Or is it building long-term relevance again?
Save this post.
Share it with your team.
And tell us where you see turquoise heading next.
Image Credits - @jckevents @juliachafe
#DiamondWorld #GemSphere #TurquoiseJewellery #ColoredGemstones #JewelleryTrends

posts.caption:
Satya Paul just unveiled its first collection under Aseem Kapoor’s creative direction on the grand staircase of Lancaster House in London. Yes, THAT Lancaster House. The one from The Crown. Think of this as a “teaser trailer” for the larger Satya Paul overhaul currently underway.
The prints, once largely confined to florals, have now been swapped for archival excavations that feel far more dangerous. Exotic polka-dotted snakes, tiger and zebra slashes, fish-scale motifs, and an archival peacock-feather print drifting across delicate silks and chiffons.
The presentation also had menswear, and under Aseem it meant tailored separates layered over flowy drapes and the same print overload as the women’s line. Fabrics were pulled, twisted, and gathered in every possible direction, while saris and scarves confetti-ed across the body, styled by Nikhil D. Headbands, capes, and kaftans gave the whole thing a louche, vintage-Italian-Pucci-by-way-of-South Delhi energy.
The Satya Paul woman is no longer an ingénue. She’s now a country-club girlie whose husband has gone suspiciously missing somewhere near Lake Como hehe.
I spoke to a bunch of women after the show who said they’d wear everything straight off the runway. A true desirability marker if you may. Still need to see more of Aseem’s long-term vision for the house, but the appetite is clearly there. That’s all!

posts.caption:
Remember Anisha? “Meri agency, meri reputation” etc? What a role. @iamkitugidwani bodied it!!
Because of the advent of A.I. and everything slowly flattening into code, prompts, algorithms, dashboards, and optimisation-slop, culture is weirdly circling back to the oldest currency of all: taste. Actual taste. Scary! The intangible thing you cannot automate no matter how many Midjourney or Claude prompts you generate. Which is why the women in branding, fashion, media, luxury, beauty, production etc, all the so-called “marketing girlies”, are emerging as the final arbiters of culture. The gatekeepers of what’s in, what becomes aspirational, what becomes cringe, what enters the group chat, and more.
Brands now shape culture more than institutions do, and the people shaping those brands inevitably become cultural architects. Meanwhile the finance bros, growth hackers, optimisation kings, LinkedIn hustle philosophers lol, and low-octave voice-using male podcasters have decided they too are now “creative directors” because they discovered the words storytelling and community in a PPM deck.
So girlies, are y’all reclaiming the “marketing girly” aka women in SPAM or no?

posts.caption:
Divyanshu Joshi, a 26-year old Delhi-based model, drowned while on location prior to an ad shoot for Kartik Research. He was also a former retail manager for the brand.
The tragedy occurred at Pettamala, a stone quarry that was shut down decades ago after multiple deaths. While the quarry is off limits, locals reportedly enter the area sometimes for bathing, washing clothes, or recreation. It is known for scenic beauty but also for dangerously deep pools which have been called ‘death traps’ because of low temperatures and an overgrowth of aquatic vegetation. In 2017, three students drowned in pools so deep that scuba divers sent to recover the bodies found the conditions challenging.
Kartik Research, the popular Delhi-based brand that was an LVMH semi-finalist in 2023, planned to do an ad shoot at Pettamala the following day, and was scouting the location when Divyanshu apparently entered the water, got out, and then went back in, slipping suddenly from the shallow edge into a deep chasm. Video taken of the incident shows the young model struggling to stay afloat. The brand had not acquired permission to shoot at Pettamala, and reports of the incident have varied, with some claiming that the brand’s founder dove in to rescue Divyanshu.
“As inaccurate accounts of this tragedy continue to circulate, we wish to clarify that Divyanshu was in Kerala as part of a seasonal photo shoot and was not participating in any swimming related activity as part of the production,” Kartik Research posted on their Instagram this week.
Many in the fashion industry are noting how the incident reflects their personal experiences on set. Safety is often overlooked on photoshoots, especially with smaller indie brands that are tasked with generating powerful creative despite budgeting and time constraints. How many fashion shoots take place everyday in impromptu or even illegal locations? How many times has a model neglected to advocate for their own health and safety (mental or physical) because they worried it would reflect poorly on them?
Divyanshu’s tragic and untimely death should be a wake up call to the industry.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ™ on June 03, 2026. May be an image of parasol, magazine, dress, flag, headdress and text that says 'Can Selkie Reclaim the US Flag During Pride Month? The brand known for costumey princess and prairie dresses isi in hot water after releasing images of its new stars-and-stripes collection. Diet PRA PRADA DA'.

posts.caption:
Selkie just released imagery for a new collection inspired by the American flag.
Fans of the brand are confused; How could Selkie—which has always been a vehicle for LGBTQ+ voices, plus-size advocacy, diversity, and women’s liberation—put out a patriotic line during a time when patriotism is impossible to untangle from fascism?
To Selkie, that was the intention.
“We refuse to surrender the symbols of this country to those who would use them against us,” reads the Instagram caption below a carousel of women in ruffled, patinated stars-and-stripes dresses.
That is, Selkie wants to reclaim the flag. In their posts, they cite how suffragettes reclaimed stars-and-stripes by sewing customized flags celebrating states where women could vote. This is a tough sell; The flag is difficult to ‘reclaim’ if it was historically wielded against you, as in the case of slaves uprooted from their ancestral countries or native tribes whose country was uprooted from beneath their feet.
There have been instances throughout fashion history when oppressive, colonialist flags were reclaimed for the sake of rebellion. The Union Jack, for example, was used by punks in the 70s to criticize the value systems of the establishment. Vivienne Westwood’s ‘God Save The Queen’ t-shirts quoted Sex Pistols lyrics to represent London punk culture during Queen Elizabeth’s “Silver Jubilee” celebration. The look was imbued with irony and featured the queen’s face over lyrics like “there is no future in England’s dream.”
Does the design of Selkie’s collection inspire rebellion? For many viewers, it appropriates antebellum style without necessarily calling it to question. It also brings to mind Elena Velez’s controversial ‘Gone With the Wind’ AW24 show which had many of the same underpinnings, but with an alt-right twist. Because of this misstep, fans are beginning to view the entire Selkie image in a new light. That is to say that they’re connecting violence with American nostalgism in ways that Selkie, a purveyor of 18th-19th century prairie dresses, formerly circumvented.
Can Selkie reclaim the flag for the girls and the gays?

posts.caption:
Choupette has yet to receive her $1.5 million inheritance from her former owner, Karl Lagerfeld, who died in 2019.
Following Lagerfeld’s death, the blue-cream Birman was left in the care of Lagerfeld’s housekeeper, Françoise Caçote, who lives in a Paris apartment with her family and another cat who is a rescue. The money left to Choupette would help Caçote support Choupette, but French law prohibits animals from inheriting. Caçote said she is “doing her best” to honor Lagerfeld’s wishes.
Mr. Lagerfeld’s wishes can’t be easy to honor. In life, his love for Choupette was notorious, even going so far as to suggest he would marry Choupette if it were legal. Lagerfeld’s icy demeanor was melted by Choupette’s enormous blue eyes, which were the inspiration for a Shu Uemura makeup line and a Chanel collection. He described the cat as “the center of the world.”
But, Choupette was not just the beloved pet of a legendary designer. She was a working model in her own right. It has been reported that Choupette made more than $3 million as a model in 2014 alone, putting her in league with top models of that year including Cara Delevingne and Kendall Jenner. Choupette flew on private jets and cavorted with the world’s biggest celebrities. She appeared on ‘The Kardashians’ in 2023, hissing and clawing at Kim Kardashian who had hoped to take her as a date to the Met Gala.
Among Choupette’s many lavish possessions (she had two hired caretakers and her own iPad) her favorites were most likely her custom Goyard feeding bowls. Sotheby’s described the bowls as “a silver-plated dish with a beaded rim and a rupee representing Queen Victoria in profile” alongside “a second one with a gilt inside” and a third “resting on a tripod base, the interior gilt and engraved CHOUPETTE, topped by a parrot,” all of which sat upon matching silver trays. The pieces were auctioned off as a set for between €300 and €400.
Choupette’s fall from grace is an undeniable tragedy to everyone who knew her in her prime. Will justice be served for her? Or is she doomed to a life as a fallen regency?

posts.caption:
As AI continues to seep into fashion, some brands are leaning on the tech to create ads, including the models in them. Last year, H&M created “digital twins” of thirty models for a campaign (boooo). Similarly, Nike used AI for a campaign featuring Serena Williams playing a tennis match against herself (boooo again). Now, a lawsuit is questioning the ethics of AI-generated models, this time involving fast-fashion retailer Rainbow Shops.
NYC–based model Francheska Pujols alleges the company hired her for a catalog shoot, then used those photos to generate entirely new images, placing her in different settings, changing her poses, and even adding other models, all without her consent.
One image highlighted in the complaint, filed May 22 in New York Supreme Court, shows that an image of Pujols standing in front of a white backdrop was altered to make the model straddle a barstool. She called the image “crude,” saying it damaged her reputation.
According to the contract which reportedly expired in March, Rainbow Shops was only allowed  to make minor edits, not create new images.
Pujols claims she sent a cease-and-desist to the brand, which was allegedly ignored, and that the images continued to circulate on the company’s website as well as in online and in-store ads.
She has since dropped the suit. Her lawyer said in a statement that the parties are “seeking to resolve this matter privately.”
Last June, advocacy nonprofit Model Alliance helped pass the Fashion Workers Act in NY, which includes protections against AI use for scenarios exactly like this. Seems like Rainbow didn’t care until Pejols got lawyered up, funny how that works.
It’s bad enough AI is stealing land and water, now we have to worry about them stealing our likenesses? Really hoping more states adopt similar protections for models, this is ridiculous!
#model #fashion #shop #style #news

posts.caption:
Euphoria’s final episode aired last night. Now that it’s over, it’s worth looking back and considering what a massive impact it had in fashion.
The show debuted in 2019 and was explosively polarizing. But what held the dicey material together was its distinct attention to styling, makeup art, and Tumblr-y production design that oscillated between hazy, grainy environments and hallucinogenic visuals. Much of the visual language can be credited to the photography of Petra Collins who worked on the show as the director before being fired by HBO.
Hunter Schafer’s character Jules was a particular touchpoint. Her appearance on television coincided with increased visibility on trans issues, particularly for teens. Jules provided a face to scores of young trans people who embraced ravey, coquetteish, anime-inspired aesthetics. Viral 2020s trends like the Lirika Hitoshi strawberry dress seem connected to Jule’s influence.
Alexa Demie’s Maddy had a fast-fashion, Revolve-esque style that prioritized sex-appeal for the male gaze without compromising her ‘slay’ for the female gaze; an ethos that seems especially potent to a generation who grew up on Instagram. Her BFF Kat, played by Barbie Ferreira, offered rare plus-size representation and a Y2K alt-girl flavor that was both the result of and catalyst for a push toward size-inclusivity.
Before Euphoria, the style of the late 2010s gave minimalism and athleisure. Going into 2020, the show gave fans permission to experiment with creative, individualized makeup and bold styling. TikTok fueled hyper trends (This-core! That-core!) appeared. The first seeds of the Indie Sleaze revival were planted, and the more laidback crowd followed Rue’s cool tomboy lead in oversized tees and cargo shorts à la Billie Eilish.
Now nearly every Euphoria actor has become a veritable style icon in real life. And, onscreen, the final season featured who its troubled teens grew up to become: Young adults who never tone down their style for anyone—because they’ve grown up in a horrifically tumultuous sociopolitical landscape, so why NOT go all out?—but who, by contrast, will never let go of their favorite old hoodie when they need comfort.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

#ELLECoverStar: Between takes, Zinnia Rose (@zinniakumar) speaks about working with domestic violence and coercive control survivors, particularly within South Asian communities. About helping women reclaim confidence, voice, and agency within legal systems that often fail to recognise the complexity of their experiences.
“I haven’t spoken about this publicly before, but I spent ten years in an abusive, financially and coercively controlling marriage,” she shares. “It shaped how much of myself I could express. Reclaiming myself and healing has been a long process, and one of the first things I did was remove the name he gave me. I’m returning to my birthright, as Zinnia Jothi Rose.”
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
On 28 May, the European Commission fined Temu under the Digital Services Act — the second-largest DSA penalty ever issued, after X got hit for €120 million last year.
The headline is dangerous products: baby toys with chemicals over safety limits, chargers that failed basic tests. But look at what the Commission’s mystery shopping exercise also found: clothing made with banned chemicals. Temu is one of the EU’s most-used fashion platforms. This is a fashion story.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
“Sustainable fashion is expensive!”: that’s something we hear *a lot*—and it’s not always true 👀⁠
⁠
Not only are there affordable secondhand and vintage options for one’s wardrobe, but there are also many brands whose prices are affordable enough, serving as a counterbalance to all sustainable fashion being “expensive”.⁠
⁠
But why are some sustainable brands more expensive? The short answer is you’re paying for better values: materials that are lower impact may require more resources while workers are paid higher wages all as intensive but cheap processes are avoided, all of which harm the planet and animals. When a price is “cheap”, the brand’s values that follow are similarly as cheap too: keep that in mind 💭⁠
⁠
Share with a friend to help spread the word on affordable and sustainable fashion. Find the full list at the link above ↑⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #Sustainability #DiscountCode #Discount #SaleSaleSale
posts.alt_text: Photo by Good On You: sustainability ratings on January 21, 2026.

posts.caption:
Ultra fast fashion brands? They’re likely using forced labour.⁠
⁠
While it’s well known that ultra fast fashion brands are cranking out cheaply made plastic clothes designed for landfills, its sometimes neglected that real people are responsible for making these garments. They go unnamed and unknown, as brands like SHEIN and Temu do nothing to support them—and are actively trying to prevent shoppers from realizing this 😡⁠
⁠
This is a reminder to ditch these brands, and any brand that isn’t taking the support of garment workers seriously. Do you consider the treatment of workers when shopping? You should: visit the link to learn more about this issue, and tag a friend to help raise awareness of this issue 👥↑⁠
⁠
#Temu #SHEIN #HM #Asos #Zara

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ’s @zachbaron, “Every single location on this movie would’ve been the hardest location on any other movie I’ve ever done… Every time we’d go somewhere, we’d be like, ‘Well, Iceland will be easier.’ And then it’s raining sideways and it’s fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‘Yeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.’”
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: “All of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren’t difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.”
Damon unwraps the ‘gift’ that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ’s summer issue cover story at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous.
Pattinson’s part in The Odyssey is not big—he plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon’s Odysseus is away. “My character’s a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,” Pattinson said. “He’s like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‘I really want to have leopard underpants.’”
Pattinson talks to GQ’s @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland’s commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). “I said to Chris, like, ‘Look, I want to do this movie, but if I’m going to do it, I’m going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,’ ” Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
“Chris has that reputation of ‘This movie isn’t going to go five months over, and we aren’t actually going to lose Tom for two years,’ ” Holland said. “Any other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.” The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he’s approaching the future of his career at the 🔗 in bio.

posts.caption:
Lisa Eldridge new limited-edition make-up collection pays homage to one of Hollywood’s most enduring beauty icons - Marilyn Monroe.
The range was created in collaboration with the Marilyn Monroe Estate to celebrate the milestone centenary of Marilyn’s birth, and at its heart? Norma Jeane, a vibrant red-orange lipstick inspired by a shade owned by Marilyn herself - and one which resides in Eldridge’s vast personal vintage make-up collection. Meticulously researched and reimagined in a modern, refillable formula, it captures the star’s signature colour, timeless glamour and ‘joie de vivre’.
Click the link in the bio to find out more

@silenceplease has teamed up with @adidasoriginals on a site-specific sound installation and limited-edition apparel capsule to celebrate the opening of the new Adidas Originals flagship store in SoHo.
At the center of the collaboration is a permanent, oversized horn-loaded speaker installation designed by Silence Please and integrated into the store's architecture. Alongside the installation, the pair have released a limited run of 100 Firebird track jackets featuring custom embroidery that reproduces the exact horn geometry of the speaker system as a technical wireframe graphic across the back. The jackets are available exclusively through Silence Please's website and at its listening room on the Bowery in New York City.

The @virgilabloharchive x @nike World Cup capsule for the USMNT is a reminder of the late Abloh’s unique ability to blend sport and fashion into something bigger than merchandise. ⁠
⁠
Part of Nike’s wider X2 World Cup collection, the collection pulls from classic tournament nostalgia, pairing bold football graphics with that signature preppy, Ralph-coded styling. We had a chance to get an up-close look at the range, which is slated to drop on June 16. ⁠

You know @heronpreston as one of streetwear’s defining figures. But beyond his namesake label, there’s @led__studio the designer’s sustainability-focused project dedicated to giving discarded materials a second life.⁠
⁠
For its latest collaboration, L.E.D. Studio teams up with multidisciplinary designer @rishiassar to transform retired New York City road signs into functional furniture. Working with the city’s Department of Transportation, the duo sourced decommissioned stop, speed limit, and school crossing signs, cutting and reassembling them with metal rivets into colorful hybrid objects that sit somewhere between a chair, stool, nightstand, and table. Following a presentation at Lichen NYC, the limited-edition stools are now available online for $1,000.⁠

⁠
Coming in 2027.⁠

posts.caption:
For Summer 2027, @zegna wants to expand your idea of what summer dressing can be.
Sure, there was plenty of linen. But @alessandrosartoriofficial’s latest collection at Malibu Pier proved warm-weather wardrobes don’t have to stop there. Leather shirts, tactile knits, striped tailoring, crocodile, suede and pops of aquamarine, orange, green and blue all found their place within Zegna’s vision of La Villeggiatura, an Italian tradition centered around relocating life, not just escaping it, for the season.
The result felt less like vacation dressing and more like a complete summer wardrobe. One built around ease, but never at the expense of elegance.
Swipe for a closer look at some of our favorite details from the show.

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer’s buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‘Pretend Cooking Show’ into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock’s adaptation of @elinhilderbrand’s bestseller ‘The Five-Star Weekend,’ the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls’ weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being “total besties” with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle’s Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine’s bio now.

posts.caption:
Yes, it’s L’OFFICIEL! Cosa hanno in comune “Dune: Part Two”, “Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga” e la nuova serie Apple TV “Lucky”? Sono tutti ambientati nel deserto—e tutti vedono protagonista @AnyaTaylorJoy. «Mi diverte sempre, perché non capisco cosa ci sia nei miei capelli, nel mio incarnato o nella larghezza dei miei occhi che mi renda adatta a interpretare una creatura del deserto. Eppure la gente adora mettermi lì», confessa Taylor-Joy a L’OFFICIEL. «Avverto una vera affinità con questi mondi».
Dal pianeta brullo di Arrakis in “Dune” al Wasteland che domina “Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga”, la nostra protagonista di giugno continua a ritrovarsi negli scenari più ostili del cinema contemporaneo. «Il deserto possiede una sacralità propria», osserva. Alla domanda su cosa ami di questi spazi desolati, la risposta è immediata: «L’alba e il tramonto, soprattutto. Perché vedi i colori più straordinari e senti di essere l’unica persona sulla terra».
Global Chief Content Officier @giampietrobaudo

posts.caption:
Introducing our global cover star, @anyataylorjoy.
“I’ve always been nomadic by nature,” the British-Argentine actor tells L’OFFICIEL. “Now I’ve just fully embraced it as part of who I am. If I have a couple of days off, I’m always like, ‘Ooh, what’s within driving distance?’ If it’s six hours or less, I’m like, ‘Let’s go. Why not?’”
Fresh off of a recent desert road trip, Taylor-Joy—who has cemented her status as a fashion darling through ambassadorships with @Dior and @TiffanyandCo—is preparing for the release of Apple TV’s ‘Lucky,’ a thriller about a con artist caught in the fallout of a multimillion-dollar heist gone wrong. The role marks another chapter in a career spanning ‘The Witch,’ ‘The Queen’s Gambit,’ ‘Furiosa,’ and ‘Dune.’
In ‘Lucky,’ her character is pursued by the FBI and a ruthless mob boss while searching for something more elusive than freedom: stability. “I was so attracted to playing somebody who was just so desperate to stay still and put down roots,” says Taylor-Joy.
Filmed across the desert and inside Las Vegas’s Caesars Palace, the production left its mark. “I have a real soft spot for Caesars Palace now—we’ve been through a lot together,” she laughs.
At the link in bio, read our full conversation with Taylor-Joy on inhabiting new characters, life on the road, and ‘Lucky.’

posts.caption:
Un nuovo capitolo nella storia della scultura contemporanea prende vita a Pietrasanta. Il 6 giugno, il @museomitoraj apre le sue porte al pubblico con “Mitoraj. Present”, la mostra inaugurale curata da @frankboehm_ e dedicata all’eredità di Igor Mitoraj, le cui opere monumentali hanno plasmato spazi pubblici in tutto il mondo. Ospitato nell’ex mercato cittadino, un iconico edificio modernista riprogettato da @obr.eu, il museo riunisce sculture in marmo, bronzo e resina, insieme a una straordinaria collezione di gioielli, in uno spazio che celebra il dialogo tra arte, architettura e artigianato.
Foto@museomitoraj by @nicola.gnesi_studio
#MuseoMitoraj #MitorajPresent #Pietrasanta #IgorMitoraj #LOfficielArt

posts.caption:
Welcome to the June issue, where L’OFFICIEL explores travel not as movement, but as a state of mind. Our “nomadic by nature” cover star @AnyaTaylorJoy discusses her love of road trips, filming the new Apple TV series ‘Lucky’ in the Las Vegas desert, and her unique approach to learning her characters. The enigmatic photographer @SzilveszterMako, renowned for his surreal images, cinematic backdrops, and dreamlike scenes, turned his lens on the actress, capturing her shifting world for our shoot.
Elsewhere, the issue focuses on slow travel and the art of pausing. We take you to Grand Cayman, Ischia, and Costa Rica, where hydrotherapy and relaxation define the experience, and across the Canadian Rockies, Arabian Desert, and Australian Outback by rail—the defining way to travel in 2026. Elsewhere, we follow one writer into the next frontier of luxury wellness: sleep optimization.
In Paris, we sit down with Hong Kong–born couturier @RobertWun, who has made a name for himself with dramatic, over-the-top silhouettes; he reminds us that slowing down is sometimes the most radical imaginative act. And in Tbilisi, it’s the creative class who are putting the Georgian capital on the map—and we have your guide to everything that’s happening now.
On the fashion front, Spring/Summer 2026’s finest looks head to Cape Town for a sun-drenched escape, while long-heralded summer heroes—unexpected denim and easy dresses—capture the spirit of going nowhere in good company, this time in Los Angeles’s canyons. Stay tuned for our issue to hit newsstands this month.

posts.caption:
For @AnyaTaylorJoy, the six-month shoot of Apple TV’s ‘Lucky’—which began shortly after the devastation of Los Angeles’s Eaton and Palisades fires in early 2025—became something unexpectedly cathartic. “There had been a conversation about not shooting in LA, but one of the things I’m most proud of is that we did keep [production] in LA and with an LA crew,” she tells L’OFFICIEL.
As both star and executive producer alongside Reese Witherspoon’s Hello Sunshine, Taylor-Joy recalls a set defined by closeness and shared experience. “I think everyone was just so happy to be with each other and working and doing something that felt constructive,” she says. “We were all kind of experiencing something, and rather than sitting in it and just being in pain, we were working together.” At the link in our bio read the full interview with Taylor-Joy.

posts.caption:
⁠⁠@AnyaTaylorJoy’s breakthrough came with Robert Eggers’s low-budget indie horror film ‘The Witch,’ which premiered at Sundance in 2015 and went on to become an A24 cult classic. Five years later, she became a household name with her Emmy-nominated role in Netflix’s lockdown-era chess phenomenon ‘The Queen’s Gambit.’ In the years since, Taylor-Joy has continued to gravitate toward projects that span genres, from horror comedy like ‘The Menu’ to period romance like ‘Emma’ to animated blockbusters like ‘The Super Mario Bros. Movie.’ ⁠
⁠
No matter the world her characters inhabit, Taylor-Joy’s process centers on complete immersion. “You spend 14 to 16 hours a day being them, thinking the way that they think, and making the choices that they make—and there can’t be any judgment. It just can’t exist,” she says. “So you have to just fully commit to whatever it is that they’re doing.” Read our full interview with Taylor-Joy as she reflects on choosing roles, following instinct, and more at the link in bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
⁠As our June issue is all about travel, it only makes sense that our cover star is someone who never stays still for long. “If I’m left to my own devices, I don’t know... I never feel as much myself as when I’m in motion, so I just sort of keep it going all the time,” @AnyaTaylorJoy tells L’OFFICIEL. ⁠
⁠
Taylor-Joy divides her time between London and Los Angeles, resisting the idea of a fixed home, and instead building a life defined by movement. At her side are two devoted companions: her rescued Doberman, Yzma (named after ‘The Emperor’s New Groove’ character, voiced by Eartha Kitt, with whom Taylor-Joy is “obsessed”), and Kitsune, a cat she found while filming ‘Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga’ who has since fully embraced a life of international travel. Both seem perfectly at ease with her ongoing state of motion. Read the full interview with Anya Taylor-Joy at the link in our bio.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
⁠What do ‘Dune: Part Two,’ ‘Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga,’ and Apple TV’s new series ‘Lucky’ have in common? They’re all set in the desert—and they all star @AnyaTaylorJoy.⁠
⁠
“It always makes me laugh, because I have no idea what it is about my hair, my skin tone, or the largeness of my eyes that makes anyone think I’m suited to be a desert creature—but people love to put me there,” Taylor-Joy tells L’OFFICIEL. “I do feel a real affinity for it.”⁠
⁠
From ‘Dune’s’ barren planet of Arrakis to The Wasteland that makes up the world of ‘Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga,’ our June cover star continues to find herself in some of cinema’s most unforgiving landscapes. “There’s a sacredness to the desert,” she says. Asked what she loves most about such desolate places, her answer is simple: “Probably sunrise or sunset, because you see the most extraordinary colors—and you just feel like you’re the only person in the world.”⁠
⁠
Read more from Taylor-Joy at the link in our bio.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.
@JacobAndCo #JacobAndCo #Luxurious #Collab

posts.caption:
At Ferrari, everything begins with the engine.
It is the heart of every car, delivering the performance, sound, and emotion that have defined the marque for decades. But a Ferrari is more than horsepower and engineering. It is the combination of design, craftsmanship, heritage, and driving passion that transforms a machine into an experience.
Every Ferrari is built to do more than move you. It is built to make you feel something.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.
Our latest issue is now live on Magzter. Read the full cover story from the link in bio.

posts.caption:
If you’ve survived K-drama-induced emotional damage, second-lead syndrome, and endless BTS playlists, South Korea might just be your perfect honeymoon destination.
From iconic filming locations, seaside cafés, dreamy islands and romantic cities you’ve seen on screen and immediately wondered, “Okay, but where is this and how fast can I get there?”. These destinations feel straight out of a K-drama.
Think incredible food, stunning views, and plenty of main-character moments. It’s romantic, exciting, and packed with places you’ve probably already fallen in love with through a screen.
Here are the destinations that deserve a spot on your honeymoon moodboard.
Link in bio🔗

posts.caption:
The bride’s girlies have all the fun! There’s shopping, outfit buying, workouts, late-night chats over coffee and matcha and some TLC at the spa, too! All that planning is sure to need some quiet catch-up and time to gather the details, sketch out timelines and get things in order. And what’s a better way to do that than at a pretty café or lovely eatery post sundown?
Throw in some fabulous food, and you have yourself some much-needed pampering. We have just those names to earmark for your glam gal time out this month. Here’s where to head with the gang…
To find out what’s worth ordering, where to go, and what makes these spots stand out, read more via the link in bio🔗
